Cardillo Documentation
  • Equations of Motion
  • View page source

Equations of Motion

This chapter documents the equations that the engine integrates for rigid bodies, point masses, and beam-derived assemblies.

Continuous form

The code organizes the dynamics in the standard constrained form

\[M(q)\,\dot{u} = f_{\mathrm{ext}}(q, u) + f_{\mathrm{int}}(q, u) + W(q)\,\lambda\]

where:

  • M(q) is the generalized mass matrix

  • u is the generalized velocity

  • f_ext includes gravity and applied external forces or torques

  • f_int covers spring, beam, and other internal contributions

  • W(q) maps contact or constraint impulses into generalized coordinates

  • \lambda is the impulse or reaction vector returned by the solver

For unilateral contact and Coulomb friction, the admissible impulses satisfy the complementarity and cone conditions

\[0 \le \lambda_n \perp g_n(q) \ge 0,\]
\[\|\lambda_t\| \le \mu\,\lambda_n\]

The actual implementation uses the available contact rows and friction model from the collision assembly, but the mathematical structure is the same.

Moreau theta update

The engine uses a Moreau-style theta step. The configuration is first drifted to the midpoint evaluation point, the state-dependent terms are assembled there, and the solver computes the velocity correction. In compact form:

\[q_{n+\theta} = q_n + (1 - \theta)\,\Delta t\,u_n\]
\[M(q_{n+\theta})\,(u_{n+1} - u_n) = \Delta t\,f(q_{n+\theta}, u_n) + W(q_{n+\theta})\,\Lambda\]
\[q_{n+1} = q_{n+\theta} + \theta\,\Delta t\,u_{n+1}\]

This matches the implementation in MoreauIntegrator::step and the pipeline’s midpoint assembly.

Rigid bodies and point masses

Rigid bodies store translation and orientation together with linear and angular velocity. Point masses are the translational special case. Both are assembled through the same world and solver infrastructure, which is why the engine can treat them uniformly in the output and tracking systems.

Beam and spring terms

Beam segments are represented as ordinary rigid entities linked by beam constraints. Their internal force contributions are assembled from the current midpoint geometry, so the equations remain consistent with the positions used for contact detection.

If you add a new mechanical model later, document it here by stating its generalized coordinates, its force law, and where it enters the residual.
